questions.question: Moses and Aaron told Pharaoh: 'Let My people go that they may hold a ______ in the wilderness.'

candidates.text: God does not say merely 'let them go' but 'let them go that they may hold a feast unto Me.' Freedom from Egypt is for the sake of worship; liberty's whole end is the festal assembly gathered around the Lord. And the true Feast is Christ our Pascha, sacrificed for us, that we may keep the feast not with old leaven but with sincerity and truth.
Freedom is not just escape; it is a feast. Moses says 'feast in the wilderness,' and Paul gives the click: Christ our Passover is sacrificed, so keep the feast.
candidates.source: Exodus 5:1; 1 Corinthians 5:7-8
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 5:1 names a feast in the wilderness, and 1 Corinthians 5:7-8 calls Christ our Passover and says to keep the feast.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good Pascha hook, but it lacks a usable source URL and can be tighter.

questions.question: Pharaoh responded, 'Who is the ______, that I should obey His voice?'

candidates.text: Pharaoh's 'Who is the Lord?' is not ignorance but a throne-claim: as a god-king he recognizes no name above his own. The whole plague cycle is the LORD answering that question by judgment against the gods of Egypt (Ex 12:12), so that by the end Egypt will know the very Name it pretended not to know.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Pharaoh asks, 'Who is the LORD?'—so God turns Egypt into the answer sheet. Exodus says Egypt will know the LORD, and God will execute judgment on Egypt's gods.
candidates.source: Exodus 5:2; Exodus 7:5; Exodus 12:12
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 5:2 records Pharaoh asking who the LORD is, Exodus 7:5 says the Egyptians will know the LORD, and Exodus 12:12 says God will execute judgment on Egypt’s gods.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Strong hook, but the support fields are missing and the current claim reaches beyond its source.

questions.question: How did Pharaoh respond to the demand to let Israel go?
options: He demanded a tribute of gold
He agreed and then immediately repented

candidates.text: Watch the law of the ascent: the moment God says 'let them go,' the tyrant does not loosen his grip but tightens it, piling MORE bricks onto already-bent backs. The squeeze grows heaviest precisely because liberation is now on the table. The pressure after the first 'let go' is not God's abandonment but the enemy's rage at losing his slave.
Pharaoh hears 'Let go' and slams on the gas: same bricks, no straw. The first answer to freedom was MORE work.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Vivid, but it adds an unsourced spiritual claim about enemy rage.

questions.question: Pharaoh ordered that the Israelites gather their own straw while keeping the same brick quota.

candidates.text: Hold the three S's: Same bricks, Stripped straw, Striped backs. Pharaoh keeps the quota high but strips away the means — and the cruelty is engineered to make Israel blame Moses instead of Pharaoh.
Three S's: Same bricks, Stripped straw, Sore backs. Pharaoh removed the straw but kept the score.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The three-S hook works, but the motive claim about blaming Moses is unsupported.

questions.question: What building material were the Israelites suddenly required to gather for themselves while still meeting the same brick quota?

candidates.text: Straw is the binding that holds the brick together; pull it and the work crumbles, yet the count stays full. 'Snatch the straw, keep the law' — the means removed, the quota unchanged.
STRAW = Still The Required Amount, Worse. No straw handed out; same brick count shouted out.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The straw-as-binding fact claim is not sourced and is not needed for recall.

questions.question: Who were beaten when the brick quotas were not met?

candidates.text: See the Israelite foremen crushed in the middle, taking the lash from Egyptians for a quota Egypt itself made impossible. In their bruised backs, bearing stripes for another's hardness, glimpse faintly the One who would later bear the stripes that were not His own.
Picture middle managers taking the beating: Egyptian whips above them, impossible brick quotas below them. Who got hit? The Israelite foremen.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The Christ-typology line needs source support and makes the aid longer than needed.

questions.question: In Chapter 5, conditions for Israel grew worse before deliverance began.

candidates.text: Picture the night at its blackest right before dawn breaks. Chapter 5 ends in the dark on purpose: in the soul's exodus the affliction grows heaviest at the very threshold of deliverance. The deepening groan is not God's absence but the eve of His outstretched arm.
Chapter 5 is the cliffhanger: Moses obeys, and things get worse before they get better. Dark brick-yard night comes before rescue dawn.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; It turns into devotional commentary instead of a tight memory hook.

questions.question: God reaffirms His covenant identity to Moses with the declaration, 'I am the ______.'

candidates.text: 'I am the LORD' is the divine Name (I AM THE EXISTING ONE) that anchors every promise that follows — the same self-naming spoken from the Bush. When Christ says 'Before Abraham was, I AM,' He claims this very Name. The God who simply IS cannot fail to keep covenant, for He stakes deliverance on His own eternal Being.
candidates.source: John 8:58

candidates.text: Lock the blank to the Name: 'I am the LORD.' For the bigger Bible hook, Jesus says, 'Before Abraham was, I AM.'
candidates.source: Exodus 6:2; John 8:58
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 6:2 records God saying “I am the LORD,” and John 8:58 records Jesus saying “Before Abraham was, I AM.”
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good Name hook, but it needs a real source URL and a shorter claim.

questions.question: How many major promises did God make to Israel in Exodus 6?

candidates.text: Count the 'I wills' and you get SEVEN — the number of the finished work, of rest attained. God does not promise deliverance by halves; the covenant is whole, lacking nothing, as the seven days of creation lacked nothing.
Seven 'I wills' march like a rescue countdown: bring out, rescue, redeem, take, be God, bring in, give. Pharaoh says 'No'; God says 'I will' seven times.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The seven-theme symbolism is unsourced; the count itself can be memorized more simply.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven promises God made in Exodus 6?
questions.answer: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
options: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
I will give it to you as an inheritance

candidates.text: Sort by the question: is this a gift TO me, or a blow to my captor? The seven 'I wills' are all gifts FOR Israel — bring OUT, rescue, redeem, take, be your God, bring IN, give inheritance. Destroying Egypt's gods is judgment done TO Egypt, real but never among the seven.
For Exodus 6, look for gifts TO Israel: out, rescue, redeem, take, be God, bring in, give. 'Destroy Egypt's gods' is the trap answer because it is not one of the seven 'I wills.'
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Good sorting instinct, but it adds an outside claim about Egypt’s gods.

questions.question: In the seven promises, God says 'I will ______ you with an outstretched arm' — the act tied to the price of deliverance.

candidates.text: 'Redeem' (ga'al) is kinsman-language — to buy back a relative out of slavery, the duty of the near-kinsman. God calls Himself Israel's Redeemer, their next-of-kin, which is exactly what the Word becomes in the flesh: our kinsman who ransoms us with His own blood. This Exodus word runs straight to Pascha.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: REDEEM = rescue with a price tag. God says, 'I will redeem you,' and Peter says we are redeemed with Christ's precious blood.
candidates.source: Exodus 6:6; 1 Peter 1:18-19
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 6:6 says God will redeem Israel, and 1 Peter 1:18-19 says believers are redeemed with the precious blood of Christ.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Rich theology, but the public source is missing and the wording is heavy.

questions.question: Among the seven promises, God said 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God.'

candidates.text: 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God' is the marriage-formula of the covenant, the heart of all the promises. It opens here at the Exodus and closes at the wedding of the Lamb, where God dwells with His people. The whole Exodus aims not at a land but at communion; everything else is for the sake of this one vow.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Lock it as a two-part pledge: 'My people; your God.' Exodus says it in the rescue story, and Revelation echoes it when God dwells with His people.
candidates.source: Exodus 6:7; Revelation 21:3
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 6:7 says God will take Israel as His people and be their God, and Revelation 21:3 says God will dwell with His people and be their God.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Strong covenant line, but it needs direct support and a tighter form.

questions.question: Why did the Israelites not listen to Moses when he brought God's promises?
questions.answer: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
options: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
Because they did not believe in God
Because Moses spoke in a foreign tongue

candidates.text: They did not refuse the good news; they could not receive it. The text calls it 'shortness of spirit' — the breath knocked out of them by hard labor. Picture a worker so winded by the whip that hope itself cannot lodge in his lungs. Cruel bondage, not unbelief, stopped their ears.
Hope hit a wall: their breath was crushed by slave labor. Picture people so beaten down that even good news cannot get past the bruises.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The breathing image is strong, but “not unbelief” is an interpretive add-on.

questions.question: The genealogy in Exodus 6 traces the lineage of which three sons of Israel?

candidates.text: The genealogy marches Reuben, Simeon, Levi — then stops dead at Levi. It is no full family tree but a zoom lens, racing past the first two sons to plant the camera on Levi, because the whole point is to certify the priestly bloodline of Moses and Aaron. Count to three and rest at Levi.
R-S-L: Reuben, Simeon, Levi. The list sprints to Levi because Moses and Aaron are coming from that line—third name, target reached.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The zoom-lens idea is helpful, but the priestly-bloodline claim is unsupported.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT one of the three sons of Levi?

candidates.text: Levi's three sons march in order Gershon, Kohath, Merari — hold 'G-K-M,' the three Levitical clans who carry the Tabernacle. Amram belongs to the next rung of the ladder, son of Kohath, not of Levi; he is a grandson where the question asks for sons.
Levi's sons are G-K-M: Gershon, Kohath, Merari. Amram is Kohath's kid, so he is Levi's grandson—not Levi's son.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The G-K-M core is good, but the Tabernacle-carrying claim goes beyond the row.

questions.question: Which son of Levi was the father of Amram (and thus ancestor of Moses and Aaron)?

candidates.text: Kohath is the hinge of the holy line — Levi, then Kohath, then Amram, then Aaron and Moses. His clan will one day bear the Ark itself on their shoulders. Remember 'Kohath the conduit,' the channel through which prophet and priest will come.
Levi → Kohath → Amram → Moses and Aaron. Kohath is the K in the middle of the family chain.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The family chain works, but the Ark claim needs sourcing.

questions.question: Who was the father of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

candidates.text: One father, three deliverers: Amram begets Aaron the priest, Moses the prophet, and Miriam the prophetess who leads the women in song. 'Amram's army of three' — one household supplying Israel's whole command.
Amram = A-M-M dad: Aaron, Moses, Miriam. One father, three famous siblings.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The sibling hook is good, but Miriam’s title adds an outside claim.

questions.question: Who was the wife of Amram and mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

candidates.text: Hear the divine Name hiding in hers: JO-chebed carries the theophoric 'Yo-/Yah' — Jochebed means 'the LORD is glory.' She is the one mother whose very name bears God's Name, fitting for the woman who bore and hid the deliverer. Jochebed = God's-Name-mother.
JocheBED: put the baby-answer to bed—Moses' mother is Jochebed. Amram + Jochebed = the Moses house.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The name-meaning claim needs a source and is not necessary for memorization.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a child of Amram and Jochebed?

candidates.text: The three of one womb are Aaron, Moses, Miriam (A-M-M). Elisheba enters the house by marriage as Aaron's WIFE, not Jochebed's daughter; watch for the test that swaps a daughter-in-law for a sister.

questions.question: Who was the wife of Aaron?

candidates.text: Elisheba IS Elizabeth. The wife of the first high priest bears the name that will one day belong to the mother of the Forerunner (herself of Aaron's daughters, Lk 1:5) — a name woven twice into the priesthood, binding the old covenant priestly line to the herald of the new.
candidates.source: Luke 1:5

candidates.text: EliSHEba—watch the SHE: she is Aaron's wife. The question asks for the woman beside Aaron, not one of his sons.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Luke 1:5 does not support the equivalence claim about Elisheba and Elizabeth.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a son of Aaron?

candidates.text: Aaron's four sons are Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. Amram cannot be among them, for he stands a generation above as Aaron's father; the line runs Amram down to Aaron, never up. He is root, not branch.

questions.question: How many sons did Aaron have, as listed in Exodus 6?

candidates.text: Four sons stand at the altar: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. A tragedy in two halves — two will fall consumed for offering strange fire, and the line survives through the other two. Four given, two faithful, the priesthood preserved by mercy.
Aaron's altar lineup has four names: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. Count N-A-E-I: four priestly sons, not three.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The later strange-fire history is outside the row and unsourced here.

questions.question: Name the two sons of Aaron whose names begin with vowels and through whom the priesthood continued.

candidates.text: Picture the four sons in line: the two elder, Nadab and Abihu, fall consumed for offering strange fire before the Lord. The priesthood carries on through the two who remain, Eleazar and Ithamar — both names opening with a vowel. The vowel-pair endures where the consonant-pair falls.
The vowel pair is E and I: Eleazar and Ithamar. Think 'E-I, E-I'—the two vowel-start names are the answer.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The vowel trick is good, but the strange-fire history needs sourcing.

questions.question: God said, 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply My signs and My ______ in the land of Egypt.'

candidates.text: The hardening cuts both ways: the text says Pharaoh hardens his own heart, and God hardens it — the same divine pressure that softens a yielding heart only bakes a defiant one harder, as the selfsame sun melts wax but bakes clay to stone. The 'wonders' multiply because Pharaoh keeps choosing the furnace, making his resistance a theater of God's glory before all nations.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Hard heart, huge wonders. Pharaoh's heart becomes a stone stage, and God piles up signs and WONDERS on it—remember the blank is WONDERS.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The hardening analogy and glory claim are teaching claims without a public source here.

questions.question: Aaron was 83 years old when he and Moses stood before Pharaoh.

candidates.text: 'Aaron Ate 3 more' — A-8-3. The elder brother is three years past 80. The mouth that speaks for God is the younger; the hand that lifts the rod is older.
Aaron Ate 3 more = A-8-3. Moses was 80, Aaron was 83: the older brother is the 83-year-old.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; The A-8-3 hook is good, but the last line muddies the point.

questions.question: How old was Moses when he stood before Pharaoh?

candidates.text: Moses' life is three forties: 40 a prince in Egypt, 40 a shepherd in Midian, 40 a shepherd of Israel. He stands before Pharaoh at 80 — two ages spent, the last and greatest just beginning.
80 = eight decades: picture Moses, 80 years old, standing straight in Pharaoh’s throne room. Pharaoh sees old; God sends bold.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The three-forties chronology is memorable but unsourced; use a cleaner 80 hook.

questions.question: When Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, what did it become?

candidates.text: The rod becomes a serpent — and the serpent, the rearing cobra, is the very emblem upon Pharaoh's crown. God lets the symbol of Egyptian sovereignty and of the ancient deceiver rise up only to be swallowed whole, showing the powers of Egypt and the serpent of old already subject to Him.
Aaron drops the rod; it drops Pharaoh’s jaw — a stick turns into a serpent right on the throne-room floor.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Cobra-crown and serpent-typology claims need sources and make the aid too loaded.

questions.question: The Egyptian magicians were able to replicate the sign of the rod becoming a serpent.

candidates.text: Picture the court sorcerers smugly tossing down their rods, each writhing into a snake by their secret arts — real powers, not sleight of hand — only to watch Aaron's serpent gulp them all down. They could mimic the marvel but never master it.
The magicians copy the sign: rods down, snakes up. But copying a sign is not controlling it — Aaron’s serpent is still the boss.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; “Real powers, not sleight of hand” is an unsourced claim beyond the answer.

questions.question: What happened after the magicians' rods also became serpents?
questions.answer: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
options: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
All the serpents turned back into rods

candidates.text: 'Aaron's snake ate the fake snakes.' The first confrontation sets the whole pattern: Egypt's powers produce a counterfeit, but God's true sign devours it whole — a preview of every plague duel to come.
Snake-eating-snake: Aaron’s serpent swallows the whole magic show. Remember it as: God’s rod eats Egypt’s rods.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; “Fake snakes” may confuse because the text says their rods became serpents; the plague-duel preview is unsourced.

questions.question: What was the first plague God brought upon Egypt?

candidates.text: Egypt called the Nile a god (Hapi) and drank life from its flood; God turns that very river to blood, judging the idol at the fountain of its life. The opening blow lands squarely on Egypt's chief deity (Ex 12:12), and the 'god' of Egypt bleeds — each plague toppling a trusted god in turn.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Egypt trusted the Nile for life; Hapi personified its flooding and fertility. In plague one, the life-river bleeds — a sharp reminder that the Lord judges Egypt’s gods.
candidates.source: Encyclopaedia Britannica, Hapi; Bible Gateway, Exodus 12:12
candidates.sourceClaim: Britannica identifies Hapi as the personification of the Nile’s annual inundation and as a fertility figure, and Exodus 12:12 says the Lord executed judgment on Egypt’s gods.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good teaching hook, but the Hapi/idol-judgment claim needs a public supporting source.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT among the waters struck in the first plague?

candidates.text: Struck: Nile, Streams, Canals, Ponds, Reservoirs — every fresh water Egypt drank, even what was already drawn into vessels. The sea is NOT in the list; the salt water no one drinks is spared, kept for a later and greater drowning.
Fresh-water checklist: river, streams, canals, ponds, reservoirs — even vessels. The sea is the odd one out: S = skipped.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The later-sea foreshadowing and drink-water framing add unsupported context.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a result of the first plague?
questions.answer: The land was covered in darkness
options: The land was covered in darkness
The Egyptians could not drink the water

candidates.text: Results of plague one: fish die, river stinks, no man can drink — all flowing from the bloodied water. Darkness is a different blow entirely, the NINTH plague, far down the road; keep blood by the river and darkness over the land apart.

questions.question: How many days did the first plague (water turned to blood) last?

candidates.text: Seven full days the Nile ran red — a complete week-long humiliation of Egypt's river-god before the next blow even begins. 'Seven days of dread by the river of red.'
Seven days, one whole week: the Nile stays red long enough for every day on the calendar to smell it. Remember: seven days of dread by the river of red.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The river-god humiliation claim needs a source; the seven-day hook can stand alone.

questions.question: The Egyptian magicians were able to imitate the first plague.

candidates.text: Picture the magicians turning a little dish of water red to save face — and so deepening Egypt's misery, not relieving it. The counterfeit can multiply the curse but cannot make one drop clean again. They add to the plague and call it power.
Magicians can copy the red-water trick, so the answer is True. They make more blood-water, not rescue-water.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; The “cannot make one drop clean” lesson is a good thought but an unsourced teaching claim.

questions.question: What was the second plague God sent upon Egypt?

candidates.text: The frog was sacred to Heqet, the frog-headed goddess of birth and fertility. God buries Egypt under her own emblem (Ex 12:12) — the symbol of teeming life becomes a teeming horror, so the fertility-goddess is shown powerless to control the very creature that represents her. Every idol is unmasked as powerless.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Heqet was the frog-shaped face of fertility; God turns that symbol into a hopping nightmare. Egypt’s “life” idol becomes impossible to live with.
candidates.source: Encyclopaedia Britannica, Heqet; Bible Gateway, Exodus 12:12
candidates.sourceClaim: Britannica describes Heqet as a frog or frog-headed goddess of regeneration, rebirth, and fertility, and Exodus 12:12 says the Lord executed judgment on Egypt’s gods.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Strong teaching aid, but Heqet and idol-judgment claims need public source support.

questions.question: Who stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt to bring up the frogs?

candidates.text: In the early plagues Moses speaks and Aaron acts: it is Aaron's hand and rod stretched over the waters that summon the frogs. 'Aaron's arm brings the swarm' — the prophet's word, the priest's outstretched hand.
Moses gives the message; Aaron gives the motion. Aaron’s arm brings the frog swarm.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; “Prophet/priest” language adds a teaching layer not needed for this recall question.

questions.question: Which of the following places did the frogs NOT invade?

candidates.text: Frogs invaded Houses, Bedrooms, Ovens, Kneading bowls — your bed and your bread defiled, no clean corner left. The temples of the gods are NOT named as a refuge; the idols cannot even keep the frogs out of their own house.
Frogs crash the house, bedroom, oven, and dough bowl — bed and bread. “Temples” is the trick option; it is not in the frog invasion list.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; Internal inconsistency: the answer says temples were not listed, but the aid implies frogs entered the idols’ own house.

questions.question: The frogs entered the Egyptians' ovens and kneading bowls.

candidates.text: Reach into the bread dough and draw out a cold, slick frog; lie down and find your bed alive. The plague seizes the most intimate places of life, the oven and the bedchamber, leaving no private corner unbesieged.

questions.question: The magicians were able to bring frogs upon Egypt.

candidates.text: The magicians could ADD frogs but never SUBTRACT a single one — a one-way power. They prove they can imitate the plague and thereby only deepen the torment; the taking-away, salvation itself, belongs to God alone.
The magicians make the frog problem worse: more frogs, not fewer. Egypt’s magic has a plus button, no minus button.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The salvation/taking-away lesson is a teaching claim without source support.

questions.question: After Pharaoh asked for relief from the frogs, what happened?
questions.answer: Moses prayed and the frogs died
options: Moses prayed and the frogs died
Aaron drove them into the sea
The frogs returned to the Nile alive

candidates.text: Watch the tell: Pharaoh begs Moses to pray, because even Pharaoh knows the magicians who conjured frogs cannot banish them. Moses prays, and the frogs die. The enemy can begin a torment, but deliverance comes by prayer alone, from God's hand only.
Pharaoh asks, Moses prays, frogs drop. Remember the order: beg, pray, dead frogs.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The prayer/deliverance lesson goes beyond recall and needs source support.

questions.question: After the frogs died, how were the dead frogs gathered?

candidates.text: Picture the dead frogs gathered into reeking heaps across the whole land, the country stinking of yesterday's plague. Even the relief leaves a stench: Pharaoh's delay turns deliverance into a mound of rot — the cost of a hardened heart lingers in the air.
Relief comes in piles: dead frogs heaped up until Egypt smells like a rotten swamp. Heaps = hills of dead frogs.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; Good image, but the hardened-heart lesson adds extra interpretation.

questions.question: Pharaoh kept his promise to let Israel go after the frogs were removed.

candidates.text: The instant the frogs die, Pharaoh forgets his vow — repentance that lasts only as long as the pain. Here is the very portrait of false turning: fear cries out under the lash and rebels the moment comfort returns. Beware repentance that breathes only in affliction and never reaches the will.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Frogs gone, promise gone. Pharaoh’s “yes” evaporates the moment Egypt can breathe again.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The false-repentance teaching lacks a verifiable public source; use a pure recall hook.

questions.question: What was the third plague, brought when Aaron struck the dust of the earth?

candidates.text: Aaron strikes the dust and the very ground breeds biting gnats — the lowliest element rising against the proud. 'Dust to dust to bugs': the third plague is born from the earth itself, not from the sky.
Dust to dust to bugs: Aaron hits the ground, and the ground hits Egypt back with gnats.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; The dust hook is strong, but the “lowliest element against the proud” line adds interpretation.

questions.question: From striking what did Aaron bring forth the lice/gnats of the third plague?

candidates.text: From the dust man was first formed, and from the dust the gnats now swarm — to bring life from dust is the Creator's own signature. That is exactly why the magicians fail here and confess 'the finger of God': they can mimic transformation but cannot create life from earth. The dust obeys only Him who first shaped it.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Dust does what magicians cannot: it turns into living gnats. Genesis says God formed man from dust; Exodus says the dust-plague makes the magicians tap out: “the finger of God.”
candidates.source: Bible Gateway, Genesis 2:7; Exodus 8:16-19
candidates.sourceClaim: Genesis 2:7 says the Lord formed man from the dust of the ground, and Exodus 8:16-19 says Aaron struck the dust, it became lice or gnats, the magicians failed, and they said, “This is the finger of God.”
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Useful creation-from-dust teaching, but it needs Scripture source support.

questions.question: The magicians were able to duplicate the third plague of lice/gnats.

candidates.text: At the third plague the imitators fail at last. 'Three and done' — they could counterfeit blood and frogs, but the gnats stop them cold, and even they must name the finger of God.

questions.question: Unable to duplicate the third plague, the magicians said to Pharaoh, 'This is the ______ of God.'

candidates.text: 'This is the finger of God' is the magicians' surrender, spoken by the enemy's own priests when their arts collapse at the gnats. That same finger will write the Law on Sinai's tablets, and Christ will cast out demons 'by the finger of God' (Lk 11:20) — where human counterfeit ends, the immediate touch of God begins.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: “Finger of God” is the magicians’ tap-out phrase: their arts hit a wall at the gnats. The same phrase marks God’s Law on tablets and Christ’s power over demons, so these three words carry serious Bible weight.
candidates.source: Bible Gateway, Exodus 8:19; Exodus 31:18; Deuteronomy 9:10; Luke 11:20
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 8:19 uses “finger of God” for the plague the magicians could not copy, Exodus 31:18 and Deuteronomy 9:10 use the phrase for the writing on the tablets, and Luke 11:20 uses it for Christ casting out demons.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good Bible-wide phrase hook, but the Sinai and Luke connections need source support.

questions.question: What was the fourth plague God sent upon Egypt?

candidates.text: After blood, frogs, and gnats comes the fourth: swarms of dog-flies filling the air. 'Four for the flies that fill the skies' — the fourth blow that darkens Egypt's sky with wings.

questions.question: During the fourth plague, which region was spared from the dog-flies (swarms of flies)?

candidates.text: 'Goshen = Go safe.' The dwelling of the people of God is the one place the dog-flies cannot enter; while all Egypt is blackened with swarms, Israel's land is sealed off untouched.

questions.question: The fourth plague marks the first time God makes a clear separation between Israel and Egypt, protecting His people while the Egyptians suffered.

candidates.text: Picture a clear line drawn across the land: dog-flies swarm everywhere in Egypt, but Goshen, where Israel dwells, stays untouched. Beginning with the flies, God 'sets apart' His own — the plagues are no blind calamity but the targeted acts of the Shepherd who knows His sheep by name, a foretaste of the Passover separation to come.
Plague four draws a giant border line: Egypt buzzes, Goshen rests. Remember the fly plague as the first “no-fly zone” for Israel.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The Shepherd/Passover typology needs source support and lengthens the aid.

questions.question: After the fourth plague, Pharaoh immediately allowed Israel to leave Egypt without conditions.

candidates.text: Watch Pharaoh negotiate: worship inside Egypt, or 'do not go far.' Every offer keeps a leash on Israel, a half-deliverance keeping them within reach of bondage — and then he hardens his heart again. The Exodus rejects the half-measure: true going-out is complete, never a managed truce with Egypt.
Pharaoh offers a leash, not liberty: worship here, or do not go far. By verse 32, the answer is still no — he hardens again.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The half-deliverance lesson is a teaching claim without source support.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct order of the second, third, and fourth plagues?

candidates.text: After blood (1): Frogs, Gnats, Flies — '1-2-3-4: Blood, Frogs, Gnats, Flies.' The hinge to remember is the gnats: the magicians keep pace through the frogs, then break at the gnats, so the order also marks where Egypt's counterfeit power dies.
